claim: Hand A's Layer-2 outers {y, n, r, ol, l} correspond distributionally to Basque case markers (ergative -k, absolutive zero, dative -ri, genitive -ren, instrumental -z, locative -n). An injective mapping from at least 3 of 5 Hand A outers to distinct Basque cases, where the Hand A outer matches the Basque case on frequency band and selectional/positional pattern, would support the Basque-like reading.
evidence_for: CAPPELLI-MATCH-01 CONFIRMED 4/5 structural matches between Hand A Layer-2 outers and Latin scribal conventions. If those are abbreviation marks over an underlying Basque text, Basque case markers should underlie the same Hand A outer positions.
AGGLUTINATIVE-SCREEN-01 matched Hand A to Basque's 12 core case markers / 5 peripheral clitics inventory scale (Hand A's 10/5 profile fits)
Basque nominal morphology is strict-slot, terminal-suffixed, and shows categorical selectional constraints — all properties Brain-V has documented in Hand A.
evidence_against: Basque case markers are multi-character (-(r)en, -(r)i, -tik) while Hand A Layer-2 outers are single-glyph. A direct one-to-one mapping is prima facie unlikely without involving Layer-1 inners in the case encoding.
Without a Basque corpus reference, 'frequency band' and 'distributional pattern' for each Basque case must be specified from published grammar literature and are approximate ranges.
The test risks the mapping being forced post-hoc. Lock all predictions before execution to prevent result-shaping.
Basque has 8+ cases; Hand A has 5 outers. Some cases will necessarily be unmatched, and that mismatch needs a principled interpretation.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  BASQUE CASE MARKER PROFILES (locked, from Hualde & Ortiz de Urbina 2003, de Rijk 2008, Laka 1996):

  C_ERG (-k, ergative, marks transitive subject):
    Terminal: yes
    Frequency band in nominal-rich text: 5-15% of word-terminal positions
    Selection: attaches to any stem type; no strong vowel/consonant preference
    Key Basque test: sigmatic — ends in a consonant (-k)

  C_ABS (zero, absolutive):
    Terminal: bare stem (no suffix)
    Frequency: typically 30-50% of tokens are bare absolutive forms
    Selection: N/A (no marker)
    Hand A analog: the fraction of tokens with NO Layer-2 outer from the set {y,n,r,ol,l}

  C_DAT (-(r)i, dative):
    Terminal: yes
    Frequency band: 3-10%
    Selection: following vowel-ending stem takes -ri; consonant-ending takes -i with linker. Overall behaviour is vowel-preferring.
    Key Basque test: ends in vowel 'i'

  C_GEN (-(r)en, genitive):
    Terminal: yes
    Frequency band: 5-15% in nominal-rich text
    Selection: vowel-ending stem takes -ren; consonant-ending takes -en. Overall vowel-preferring.
    Key Basque test: ends in consonant 'n'

  C_INST (-z, instrumental):
    Terminal: yes
    Frequency band: 2-8%
    Selection: any stem; may show slight vowel preference
    Key Basque test: ends in sibilant 'z'

  C_LOC (-n, inessive locative):
    Terminal: yes
    Frequency band: 5-15%
    Selection: vowel-ending stem takes -(a)n; consonant-ending stems are rare in locative.
    Key Basque test: ends in consonant 'n'; vowel-preferring selection

  HAND A OUTER PROFILES (from CAPPELLI-MATCH-01 output):
    y   term 0.773  freq 0.315  diff V/C 0.256   (cons-preferring)
    n   term 0.946  freq 0.155  diff V/C inf     (vowel-exclusive)
    r   term 0.799  freq 0.169  diff V/C 76.06   (vowel-preferring)
    ol  term 0.735  freq 0.143  diff V/C 0.292   (cons-preferring)
    l   term 0.549  freq 0.043  diff V/C 8.97    (vowel-preferring)
    bare (no top-5 outer): approximately 14% of N>=3 tokens (computed at execution)

  PER-OUTER MATCHING (LOCKED):
    For each Hand A outer, determine the single best-matching Basque case by evaluating:
      (1) Is the Hand A outer's frequency within the case's frequency band? (FREQ)
      (2) Does the Hand A outer's selectional profile match the case's expected selection? (SEL)
      (3) Is the Hand A outer's terminal fraction compatible with the case being a suffix? (TERMINAL >= 0.60)
    An outer-to-case match requires 2-of-3 criteria to pass.

  INJECTIVE MAPPING:
    At most one Hand A outer maps to each Basque case. If two outers both best-match the same case, only the better-matching is counted; the other is unmatched.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED: >=3 Hand A outers map to distinct Basque cases at 2-of-3 criteria each.
    MARGINAL: Exactly 2 Hand A outers map.
    REFUTED: <=1 Hand A outer maps.

  DIAGNOSTIC (non-decision-feeding):
    Report whether the Hand A 'bare' population fraction (tokens with no top-5 outer) falls within Basque's 30-50% C_ABS absolutive expected range.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if >=3 outers map injectively to distinct cases. MARGINAL if 2. REFUTED if <=1.

tests_run.details:
CONFIRMED 4/5 under letter of locked rule, BUT with significant frequency-band diagnostic failure. 8794 Hand A N>=3 tokens analysed.

  INJECTIVE MAPPING ACHIEVED:
    ol -> C_ERG (ergative -k)   score 3/3  (freq 0.143 in [0.05, 0.15]; any-selection OK; term 0.735)
    y  -> C_INST (instr. -z)    score 2/3  (freq OUT; any-selection OK; term 0.773)
    n  -> C_DAT (dative -(r)i)  score 2/3  (freq OUT; V-pref match; term 0.946)
    r  -> C_GEN (genitive -(r)en) score 2/3 (freq OUT; V-pref match; term 0.799)
    l  -> (unmatched) all remaining cases score <2/3 for l (term 0.549 fails the >=0.60 requirement for every case with SEL-pass)

  SCORING MATRIX HIGHLIGHTS: 25 outer-case combinations tested. Only 2 pass the FREQ criterion (ol-ERG and ol-GEN). The other 23 outer-case pairs all FAIL on frequency because Hand A Layer-2 outers are SYSTEMATICALLY HIGHER in frequency than any single Basque case marker.

  CRITICAL DIAGNOSTIC: bare-fraction = 0.1425.
    Hand A has only 14.25% of N>=3 tokens ending in something other than {y, n, r, ol, l}.
    Basque C_ABS (absolutive zero-marker) is expected at 30-50%.
    Hand A's bare-fraction is LESS THAN HALF the Basque ABS lower bound.

  INTERPRETATION:
    The injective mapping formally passes the locked rule at 4/5. But the mapping relies on the SEL and TERM criteria; the FREQ criterion fails in almost every case. This reveals a structural mismatch:

    Basque text has ~30-50% bare absolutive + ~50-70% case-marked forms, with no single case exceeding ~15%. Hand A has ~14% bare + ~86% top-5-outer-marked, with individual outer frequencies ranging 4% to 31%.

    Hand A is MORPHOLOGICALLY MORE SATURATED than Basque text would be. Possible explanations:
      (a) Hand A's outers encompass MORE than just the 6 tested Basque cases — e.g., the full Basque case inventory (ERG, ABS, DAT, GEN, INST, LOC, ABL -tik, ALL -ra, comitative -kin, benefactive -tzat), postpositions, and function-word clitics collapsed onto the Layer-2 position. Under this reading, Hand A outers are MULTI-FUNCTION markers, not single Basque cases.
      (b) Hand A is not Basque but a morphologically denser agglutinative system.
      (c) The scribal abbreviation layer has ALSO collapsed additional morphemes, so what appears as a single Hand A outer encodes multiple Basque morphemes.

    The y-as-INST mapping is particularly weak — y at 31.5% terminal frequency is 4x over Basque INST's 2-8% expected band. The y outer likely encodes multiple Basque morphological categories (consistent with CAPPELLI-MATCH-01's finding that y does not fit any single Latin abbreviation for the same reason).

  NET CONCLUSION: The Basque-like reading is NOT falsified by this test — every Hand A outer has a compatible SEL profile and TERM fraction for at least one Basque case. But the FREQ distribution mismatch is substantial, meaning the mapping is approximate rather than clean. A rigorous Basque-to-Hand-A correspondence would require Layer-2 outers to be understood as COMPOSITE markers encoding multiple underlying Basque morphemes — plausible under scribal abbreviation but not directly proven by this test.

verdict_note: CONFIRMED 4/5 under letter of locked rule. Injective mapping: ol->ERG (3/3), y->INST, n->DAT, r->GEN (each 2/3); l unmatched. Significant diagnostic caveat: Hand A's bare-fraction is 14.25%, far below Basque C_ABS expected range of 30-50%. Only 2/25 outer-case pairs pass the FREQ criterion — Hand A outers are systematically more frequent than Basque cases. This implies Hand A outers are likely COMPOSITE markers encoding multiple Basque morphemes (cases + postpositions + clitics), not single-case 1-to-1 matches. Consistent with the scribal-abbreviation framing but requires a richer correspondence model than Basque-case-for-Hand-A-outer. The Basque-like hypothesis survives but is not cleanly validated at the case-mapping level.
findings_for_followup: Hand A's 14.25% bare-fraction vs Basque's 30-50% ABS is the sharpest numerical disagreement in this test. Two follow-up hypotheses: (a) Hand A has an additional suffix class beyond top-5 outers that encodes absolutive — check tokens ending in 's', 'm', 'g' etc. for absolutive-like distributional profile. (b) Hand A is not Basque but a different agglutinative language where all nominal forms are obligatorily case-marked (no zero form).

The y-as-INST mapping is weakest (2/3 with freq way out). Combined with CAPPELLI-MATCH-01's finding that y doesn't fit any single Latin convention, y appears to be a COMPOSITE marker. A targeted decomposition test: partition y-final words by preceding context and check whether sub-populations have distinct distributional signatures consistent with multiple underlying morphemes.

The ol->ERG 3/3 mapping is the cleanest individual match. Basque ergative -k marks transitive subjects and has the moderate-frequency, any-selection, strictly-terminal profile that matches ol exactly. If ol really encodes ergative, Hand A's consonant-class inners + ol combinations should cluster in transitive-predicate-rich contexts. A validation test: check whether ol-final words preferentially occupy agent-like positions in the paragraph structure.

The strong n-as-DAT / n-as-GEN ambiguity reflects a real issue: both Basque cases have V-preferring selection, and n has inf V-pref. Resolving which case n really encodes (if either) would require either: (a) sub-category distinctions within n-final words based on surrounding syntax, or (b) acceptance that Hand A outers are composite.

Consider testing Hand A outer correspondence to broader Basque morphological inventory (all ~15 case markers + peripheral clitics) rather than just the core 6 tested here. A Hand A outer matching 2-3 Basque morphemes simultaneously would explain the frequency saturation.
reasoning: If Hand A encodes a Basque-like language under Latin scribal abbreviation, the observed Layer-2 outer positions are where Basque case markers would appear in a hypothetical underlying text. The test checks whether the mapping from Hand A outers to Basque cases can be made injectively on the basis of frequency and selectional properties — without looking at surface glyph shape (which would already be governed by the abbreviation-mapping, not by Basque sounds). The test is honest about its limits: with 5 outers vs 8 Basque cases, some cases will necessarily be unmatched; the question is whether the RE-COVERABLE sub-mapping shows enough internal coherence to support the Basque-like reading.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed falsification attempt on Basque-like+Latin-abbreviation convergence hypothesis; test 2 of 4
